questions.q: A gain on the early extinguishment of debt, by a company that routinely retires its own bonds, is reported:
questions.a: In income from continuing operations, before tax
wrong: Net of tax, in a section below continuing operations
As a prior period adjustment to retained earnings
questions.why: Debt extinguishment gains and losses are ordinary items shown within continuing operations at their pretax amount.

questions.q: A loss from an event that is both unusual in nature and infrequent in occurrence is now reported:
questions.a: As a separate pretax line within continuing operations
wrong: Net of tax in its own section after continuing operations
questions.why: U.S. GAAP eliminated the extraordinary-item category; such items appear within continuing operations, before tax.

questions.q: Under current U.S. GAAP, an unrealized holding gain on equity securities with a readily determinable fair value is:
questions.a: Recognized in net income as it occurs
wrong: Deferred in OCI until the securities are sold
questions.why: Equity securities are carried at fair value through net income (FVTNI), so unrealized changes hit earnings each period.

questions.q: A company shortens the estimated useful life of a machine still in service. The effect is accounted for:
questions.a: Prospectively, over the remaining useful life
wrong: As a prior period error correction
Entirely in the current period as a loss
questions.why: A change in useful life is a change in estimate, applied prospectively to current and future periods.

questions.q: Equipment costing $300,000 with no salvage value was depreciated straight-line over 10 years. After 4 years, the total useful life is revised to 6 years. The new annual depreciation is:
questions.why: Remaining book value is $180,000 ($300,000 − 4×$30,000); spread over the 2 remaining years gives $90,000.

questions.q: The allowance for doubtful accounts is reported as:
questions.a: A contra-asset reducing accounts receivable
wrong: An expense shown only on the income statement
questions.why: The allowance offsets gross receivables to net realizable value, so it is a contra-asset.

questions.q: An unamortized discount on bonds payable is presented as:
questions.a: A reduction of the related bonds payable (a contra-liability)
questions.why: Bond discount reduces the carrying amount of the bonds; it is a contra-liability.

questions.q: A $500,000 note payable requires a $100,000 principal payment within the next 12 months. On a classified balance sheet it is reported as:
questions.a: $100,000 current and $400,000 noncurrent
questions.why: The portion due within one year is current; the remainder is noncurrent.

questions.q: Treasury stock is reported as:
questions.a: A reduction of total stockholders' equity
questions.why: Treasury stock is a contra-equity account, not an asset.

questions.q: A company changes its depreciation method from double-declining-balance to straight-line. This is treated as:
questions.a: A change in estimate, applied prospectively with no catch-up
wrong: A change in principle requiring retrospective restatement
A direct adjustment to additional paid-in capital
questions.why: A change in depreciation method is a change in estimate effected by a change in principle — handled prospectively.

questions.q: An error that understated depreciation expense in a prior year not presented is corrected by:
questions.a: Adjusting the beginning balance of retained earnings in the earliest period presented
wrong: Reporting the full correction in current-year income
Ignoring it if the tax effect is small
questions.why: Prior period error corrections adjust beginning retained earnings (net of tax), not current earnings.

questions.q: Depreciation was understated by $400,000 in a prior year; the tax rate is 25%. The correction to beginning retained earnings is:
questions.a: A $300,000 decrease
questions.why: The after-tax effect, $400,000 × (1 − 0.25) = $300,000, reduces beginning retained earnings since expense was understated.

questions.q: Once a component is classified as held for sale during the year, its operating results for the entire year are:
questions.a: Reported in discontinued operations, net of tax
wrong: Split between continuing and discontinued based on the decision date
questions.why: The component's full-period results are presented in discontinued operations in the period it qualifies as held for sale.

questions.q: Anticipated future operating losses of a component held for sale are:
questions.a: Not recognized until they are actually incurred
wrong: Accrued immediately when the plan to sell is adopted
Recorded net of tax in the current year
Added to the current impairment loss
questions.why: Expected future losses are recognized as incurred, not accrued in advance.

questions.q: A division held for sale has assets of $6,000,000 and liabilities of $2,500,000; its fair value less costs to sell is $2,900,000. The impairment loss to recognize is:
questions.why: Impairment equals carrying amount of net assets ($3,500,000) minus fair value less costs to sell ($2,900,000).

questions.q: A discontinued component with a carrying amount of $3,100,000 (after a prior-year impairment) is sold for $3,400,000. The tax rate is 25%. The after-tax gain on disposal is:
questions.why: The $300,000 pretax gain is reported net of tax: $300,000 × 0.75 = $225,000.

questions.q: A U.S. company sells goods on account priced in euros. The receivable and sale are initially recorded at:
questions.a: The spot exchange rate on the transaction date
wrong: The rate in effect when cash is collected
questions.why: Foreign-currency transactions are recorded at the spot rate on the date of the transaction.

questions.q: Between the sale date and the collection date, the euro weakens against the dollar. The resulting foreign currency transaction loss is reported:
questions.a: In net income
wrong: As an adjustment to sales revenue
questions.why: Transaction gains and losses are reported in net income; translation adjustments go to OCI.

questions.q: A company issues a purchase order for raw materials priced in a foreign currency. On the order date it should:
questions.a: Make no journal entry
wrong: Record inventory and a payable at the spot rate
Record the payable at the forward rate
questions.why: A purchase order is an executory commitment; no entry is made until the purchase actually occurs.

questions.q: A U.S. firm owes €80,000. The indirect rate moves from €0.80 per $1 at the purchase date to €0.75 per $1 at year-end. The year-end adjustment is approximately:
questions.a: A loss of about $6,667
questions.why: The dollar amount owed rises from $100,000 (80,000/0.80) to about $106,667 (80,000/0.75) as the euro strengthens — a transaction loss.

questions.q: Comprehensive income is defined as:
questions.a: Net income plus other comprehensive income
questions.why: Comprehensive income equals net income plus OCI.

questions.q: Which item is reported in other comprehensive income rather than net income?
questions.a: A foreign currency translation adjustment
wrong: A gain on the sale of equipment
An unrealized gain on equity securities
questions.why: Translation adjustments are an OCI item, whereas equity-security gains now go to net income (FVTNI).

questions.q: The prior service cost arising from a pension plan amendment is initially recognized in:
questions.a: Other comprehensive income
questions.why: Prior service cost is recorded in OCI and later amortized into pension expense.

questions.q: Under the fair value option for a financial liability, the portion of the fair-value change attributable to instrument-specific credit risk is:
questions.a: Reported in other comprehensive income
wrong: Added to the liability's par value
questions.why: The credit-risk portion of the change is presented in OCI, with the remainder in net income.

questions.q: A $40,000 foreign currency translation gain is taxed at 25%. The amount reported in OCI is:
questions.why: OCI items are presented net of tax: $40,000 × (1 − 0.25) = $30,000.

questions.q: The denominator of basic EPS is:
questions.a: The weighted-average number of common shares outstanding
questions.why: Basic EPS uses the weighted-average number of common shares outstanding.

questions.q: In the numerator of basic EPS, dividends on cumulative preferred stock are:
questions.a: Subtracted whether or not they were declared
questions.why: Cumulative preferred dividends reduce income available to common shareholders regardless of declaration.

questions.q: Under the if-converted method, dilutive convertible bonds add to the numerator:
questions.a: The bond interest expense, net of tax
questions.why: Assumed conversion eliminates the after-tax interest, so it is added back to income available to common.

questions.q: A potentially convertible security is included in diluted EPS only if its assumed conversion:
questions.a: Decreases EPS (is dilutive)
questions.why: Antidilutive securities are excluded, and each issue is tested separately for dilution.

questions.q: $5,000,000 of 6% convertible bonds are outstanding for a full year; the tax rate is 40%. The after-tax interest add-back for diluted EPS is:
questions.why: Interest is $5,000,000 × 6% = $300,000; net of 40% tax the add-back is $180,000.

questions.q: A company has net income of $2,000,000 and 1,000,000 weighted-average common shares, plus cumulative preferred stock with $300,000 of annual dividends (not declared). Basic EPS is:
questions.why: Income available to common is $2,000,000 − $300,000 = $1,700,000; divided by 1,000,000 shares = $1.70.

questions.q: When a corporation issues common stock in exchange for land, the transaction is generally recorded at:
questions.a: The fair value of the stock issued, or the asset, whichever is more clearly determinable
wrong: The par value of the stock
The seller's book value of the land
questions.why: Nonmonetary issuances use the fair value of consideration given or received, whichever is more reliably measurable.

questions.q: A property dividend (for example, distributing inventory) is recorded by the payer at:
questions.a: The fair value of the property, recognizing any gain or loss to revalue it
wrong: The carrying amount of the property, with no gain or loss
The par value of the related stock
questions.why: The asset is remeasured to fair value (gain or loss recognized), and retained earnings is reduced at that fair value.

questions.q: A small stock dividend (below roughly 20–25%) is recorded by capitalizing retained earnings at:
questions.a: The fair value of the shares on the declaration date
questions.why: Small stock dividends transfer fair value from retained earnings to paid-in capital (par to common stock, excess to APIC).

questions.q: On the declaration date of a cash dividend:
questions.a: Retained earnings is reduced and a dividend payable is recorded
wrong: No entry is made until payment
questions.why: Declaration creates the liability and reduces retained earnings; cash moves only on the payment date.

questions.q: Under the cost method, reissuing treasury stock above its repurchase cost results in:
questions.a: A credit to additional paid-in capital from treasury stock (no gain in income)
wrong: A gain reported in net income
A credit to retained earnings only
questions.why: Treasury stock profits never hit income; the excess over cost is credited to APIC — treasury stock.

questions.q: Under the cost method, reissuing treasury stock below cost, when no APIC from treasury stock exists, is absorbed by:
questions.a: A debit to retained earnings
questions.why: The shortfall first reduces any APIC — treasury stock, then retained earnings; it is never a loss in income.

questions.q: A company reacquires 1,000 shares (originally issued at $8; $5 par) for $12 each using the cost method. The entry is:
questions.a: Debit treasury stock $12,000; credit cash $12,000
wrong: Debit treasury stock $5,000 and APIC $3,000; credit cash $12,000
questions.why: The cost method records treasury stock at the full reacquisition cost, $12 × 1,000 = $12,000.

questions.q: Comparing the par-value and cost methods of accounting for treasury stock, which is true?
questions.a: Total stockholders' equity is the same under both methods
wrong: Net income differs between the methods
The cost method always shows higher equity
Common stock is eliminated under the cost method
questions.why: The methods reclassify amounts among equity accounts differently but arrive at the same total equity.

questions.q: Under the five-step revenue model (ASC 606), revenue is recognized when:
questions.a: The customer obtains control of the promised good or service
questions.why: Revenue is recognized as performance obligations are satisfied — that is, when control transfers to the customer.

questions.q: A contract bundles two distinct performance obligations. The transaction price is allocated to each based on:
questions.a: Their relative standalone selling prices
wrong: The prices stated in the contract
An equal split between the obligations
questions.why: The transaction price is allocated in proportion to each obligation's standalone selling price, not the contract-stated prices.

questions.q: Under a bill-and-hold arrangement, the seller may recognize revenue before shipment only if, among other criteria:
questions.a: The product is separately identified as the customer's and ready to transfer
wrong: The customer has paid in full
The seller stores it free of charge
questions.why: Bill-and-hold revenue requires a substantive reason, the product identified as the customer's, ready to transfer, and not usable for other customers.

questions.q: A company sells equipment for $50,000 with a call option to repurchase it for $54,000. This is accounted for as:
questions.a: A financing arrangement — recognize a liability, not revenue
questions.why: Because the repurchase price exceeds the original price, the seller retains control; it is a financing arrangement, with the $4,000 difference treated as interest.

questions.q: A seller expects 30% of a $20,000 shipment to be returned. On sale it should:
questions.a: Recognize $14,000 of revenue and a $6,000 refund liability
wrong: Defer all revenue until the return window closes
questions.why: Revenue equals the amount the seller expects to be entitled to ($14,000); the expected returns ($6,000) are a refund liability.

questions.q: Cash of $24,000 is received for a two-year service contract. After year one the seller recognizes:
questions.a: $12,000 of revenue, leaving $12,000 as a contract liability
questions.why: Revenue is recognized as the obligation is satisfied; the unearned portion remains a contract (deferred-revenue) liability.

questions.q: Under the cost-to-cost method, the percentage of completion equals:
questions.a: Costs incurred to date divided by total estimated costs
wrong: Costs incurred to date divided by the contract price
Billings divided by the contract price
Time elapsed divided by total contract time
questions.why: Cost-to-cost measures progress as costs incurred to date over total estimated costs.

questions.q: A contract has a $400,000 price and $250,000 estimated total cost; costs to date are $100,000. Profit recognized to date is:
questions.why: Estimated profit is $150,000; at 40% complete ($100,000/$250,000), recognized profit is $150,000 × 40% = $60,000.

questions.q: On a cost-to-cost contract, cumulative profit through year two should be $90,000; $60,000 was recognized in year one. Year two profit is:
questions.why: Recognition is cumulative: current-period profit equals cumulative-to-date less amounts already recognized ($90,000 − $60,000).

questions.q: When the construction-in-progress balance exceeds progress billings, the net amount is reported as:
questions.a: A current asset
questions.why: CIP greater than billings is a contract asset; billings greater than CIP is a contract liability.

questions.q: A long-term contract is now expected to produce an overall loss. The expected loss is:
questions.a: Recognized in full in the period it becomes evident
wrong: Spread over the remaining contract term
Recognized only to the extent of costs incurred
questions.why: Expected contract losses are recognized immediately and in full once estimable, regardless of stage of completion.

questions.q: A change in accounting principle (for example, a change in inventory method other than to LIFO) is generally reported:
questions.a: Retrospectively, with the cumulative effect adjusting beginning retained earnings of the earliest period presented
wrong: As a current-period gain or loss
questions.why: Changes in principle are applied retrospectively unless doing so is impracticable.

questions.q: A change from FIFO to LIFO is accounted for:
questions.a: Prospectively, because computing the cumulative effect is impracticable
questions.why: Rebuilding old LIFO cost layers is impracticable, so a change to LIFO is handled prospectively.

questions.q: Switching from the income-tax basis (a non-GAAP method) to GAAP is treated as:
questions.a: A correction of an error — restate prior periods
questions.why: Moving from a non-GAAP basis to GAAP corrects an error, so prior periods are restated.

questions.q: When a company changes its reporting entity (for example, by consolidating a newly acquired subsidiary), prior comparative statements are:
questions.a: Restated retrospectively to reflect the new entity
questions.why: A change in reporting entity is reported by retrospectively restating the prior periods presented.

questions.q: The adjusting entry for wages earned by employees but unpaid at year-end is:
questions.a: Debit wages expense, credit wages payable
wrong: Debit prepaid wages, credit wages expense
No entry until the wages are paid
questions.why: Accrued expenses are recorded with an expense debit and a liability credit in the period incurred.

questions.q: A company paid $12,000 for 12 months of insurance and expensed all of it. The year-end adjustment for 9 unexpired months is:
questions.a: Debit prepaid insurance $9,000, credit insurance expense $9,000
wrong: Debit prepaid insurance $3,000, credit insurance expense $3,000
questions.why: The unexpired $9,000 (9/12 × $12,000) is a prepaid asset; only the expired portion remains an expense.

questions.q: Services were performed and billed this year, but cash will arrive next year. The adjusting entry is:
questions.a: Debit accounts receivable, credit revenue
wrong: No entry until cash is received
questions.why: Earned revenue is recognized when earned, with a receivable, regardless of when cash is collected.

questions.q: Goods shipped FOB destination are still in transit at year-end. They should be:
questions.a: Included in the seller's inventory
wrong: Recorded as a completed sale by the seller
questions.why: Under FOB destination, title passes on delivery, so in-transit goods remain the seller's inventory.

questions.q: Estimated uncollectible accounts are 3% of $10,000,000 in receivables. The bad debt adjusting entry is:
questions.a: Debit bad debt expense $300,000, credit allowance for doubtful accounts $300,000
wrong: Debit bad debt expense $300,000, credit accounts receivable $300,000
questions.why: Estimating uncollectibles records expense against the contra-asset allowance — not a direct write-off of the receivable.

questions.q: Investments in debt securities classified as held-to-maturity are reported at:
questions.a: Amortized cost
questions.why: Held-to-maturity debt securities are carried at amortized cost; fair value is disclosed but not used for measurement.

questions.q: On the balance sheet and in the notes, trade accounts receivable should be:
questions.a: Presented separately from non-trade items such as interest and dividends receivable
questions.why: Trade receivables are shown separately from non-trade receivables.

questions.q: The 'use of estimates' footnote (noting that actual results may differ from estimates) is required by:
questions.a: U.S. GAAP
questions.why: This disclosure of measurement uncertainty is a GAAP requirement, not an auditing-standards requirement.

questions.q: A company earns a large share of revenue from five customers. Disclosure of this concentration:
questions.a: Is required, but the customers need not be named
wrong: Is required, including the customers' names
questions.why: GAAP requires disclosure of significant customer concentrations without naming the customers.

questions.q: Cash that is restricted for repaying a long-term obligation is classified as:
questions.a: A noncurrent (long-term) asset
wrong: A reduction of the related liability
questions.why: Restricted cash is classified with the obligation it supports; cash restricted for long-term debt is noncurrent.

questions.q: A subsequent event is recognized (the financial statements are adjusted) when:
questions.a: The underlying condition existed at the balance sheet date
wrong: The event arose entirely after the balance sheet date
It is favorable to the company
questions.why: Recognized (Type I) events relate to conditions existing at the balance sheet date and are adjusted in the statements.

questions.q: A lawsuit pending at year-end settles for more than the recorded liability before the statements are issued. The company should:
questions.a: Adjust the year-end financial statements for the additional amount
wrong: Disclose it in the notes only
Record it entirely in the next year
questions.why: The condition existed at the balance sheet date, so it is a recognized subsequent event requiring adjustment.

questions.q: After year-end but before issuance, an investment's fair value declines due to new market conditions. This is:
questions.a: A nonrecognized subsequent event — disclose if material
questions.why: The condition arose after the balance sheet date, so it is a nonrecognized (Type II) event — disclosed, not accrued.

questions.q: A company sells a subsidiary after the balance sheet date but before the statements are issued. The sale should be:
questions.a: Disclosed in the notes as a nonrecognized subsequent event
wrong: Used to restate the prior year
questions.why: The sale arose after year-end, so it is disclosed (nonrecognized), not reflected in the year-end balances.

questions.q: In the fair value hierarchy, the highest-priority (Level 1) inputs are:
questions.a: Quoted prices in active markets for identical assets
questions.why: Level 1 uses quoted prices for identical items in active markets; unobservable Level 3 inputs have the lowest priority.

questions.q: Fair value is best described as:
questions.a: An exit price — the price to sell an asset or transfer a liability
wrong: An entry price — the cost to acquire the asset
questions.why: Fair value is a market-based exit price under current measurement assumptions.

questions.q: An asset trades in several markets, one of which is its principal market. Fair value is measured using:
questions.a: The price in the principal market, even if another market offers a better price
questions.why: When a principal market exists, its price governs fair value regardless of more advantageous prices elsewhere.

questions.q: With no principal market, the most advantageous market is identified net of transaction and transportation costs. The reported fair value then:
questions.a: Excludes transaction costs but is reduced by transportation costs
wrong: Excludes both transaction and transportation costs
Includes transaction costs but not transportation costs
questions.why: Transaction costs help identify the best market but are not part of fair value; transportation costs are deducted from the exit price.

questions.q: For a nonfinancial asset, fair value assumes:
questions.a: The highest and best use by market participants, ignoring management's intended use
questions.why: Fair value of a nonfinancial asset reflects market participants' highest and best use, not the owner's intent.

questions.q: Which asset is reported at historical cost rather than fair value?
questions.a: Land held for investment
wrong: A money market fund classified as a cash equivalent
Equity securities with a readily determinable fair value
questions.why: Land is carried at historical cost regardless of intended use; the others are financial instruments measured at fair value.

questions.q: Converting cash-basis statements to accrual, sales made on account but not yet collected at year-end are:
questions.a: Added to revenue, with a corresponding accounts receivable
questions.why: Accrual recognizes earned revenue when earned; the uncollected amount becomes a receivable.

questions.q: Under the modified cash basis, equipment that was expensed when purchased under the pure cash basis is:
questions.a: Capitalized as an asset and depreciated over its useful life
questions.why: A common modification capitalizes long-lived assets and recognizes depreciation, equivalent to accrual treatment.

questions.q: Converting to accrual, income taxes incurred but unpaid at year-end are:
questions.a: Accrued as income tax expense and income tax payable
questions.why: Accrual recognizes the tax obligation as of the balance sheet date, with any unpaid amount reported as a payable.

questions.q: The quick (acid-test) ratio differs from the current ratio in that it:
questions.a: Excludes inventory and prepaid items from current assets
wrong: Uses total assets instead of current assets
questions.why: The quick ratio uses only the most liquid current assets (cash, short-term investments, receivables), excluding inventory and prepaids.

questions.q: A company has current assets of $1,200,000 and current liabilities of $500,000. Its current ratio is:
questions.why: Current ratio = current assets ÷ current liabilities = $1,200,000 ÷ $500,000 = 2.4.

questions.q: If average inventory rises while cost of goods sold stays roughly flat, inventory turnover:
questions.a: Decreases (inventory is moving more slowly)
questions.why: Turnover = COGS ÷ average inventory; a larger denominator with a flat numerator lowers the ratio.

questions.q: Goods held by a consignee on consignment at year-end are:
questions.a: Included in the consignor's inventory
wrong: Recorded as a sale by the consignor
questions.why: Title remains with the consignor, so consigned goods stay in the consignor's inventory until sold.

questions.q: On a bank reconciliation, deposits in transit are:
questions.a: Added to the bank balance
questions.why: Deposits recorded by the company but not yet by the bank are added on the bank side; outstanding checks are deducted from it.

questions.q: A customer's check that is returned NSF (bounced) requires the company to:
questions.a: Reduce its cash balance and reinstate the receivable
questions.why: An NSF check was never good funds, so book cash is reduced and the receivable from the customer is restored.

questions.q: A check the company wrote for $740 was erroneously recorded in its books as $470. The correcting adjustment to the book balance is to:
questions.a: Decrease the book balance by $270
questions.why: The disbursement was understated by $270 in the books, so book cash must be reduced by the difference.

questions.q: A bank service fee and a note collected by the bank on the company's behalf appear on the bank statement but not the books. On the reconciliation these are:
questions.a: A deduction (service fee) and an addition (note collected) to the book balance
wrong: Both additions to the bank balance
Both deductions from the book balance
questions.why: Items the bank has recorded but the company has not are adjusted on the book side — the fee reduces cash, the collection increases it.

questions.q: Under the allowance method, writing off a specific uncollectible account:
questions.a: Reduces both the allowance and accounts receivable, with no effect on net income
wrong: Records bad debt expense at the write-off
questions.why: The write-off (debit allowance, credit A/R) was already anticipated by prior bad debt expense, so it does not affect income.

questions.q: Aging shows $80,000 over 90 days (60% estimated uncollectible) and $200,000 at 0–30 days (3% estimated). The required allowance from these two buckets is:
questions.why: The aging method applies each bucket's percentage and sums them: $48,000 + $6,000 = $54,000.

questions.q: A trade discount stated on an invoice is:
questions.a: Deducted from the gross price, so the receivable is recorded at its net amount
questions.why: Trade discounts reduce the invoice price directly, so the receivable and revenue are recorded net of the discount.

questions.q: A receivable is deemed fully uncollectible after the customer's bankruptcy, but the allowance held only part of the balance. The company should:
questions.a: Write off the entire receivable, recognizing the additional credit loss
wrong: Write off only the amount in the allowance
Leave the receivable on the books
questions.why: The full receivable is written off; any amount beyond the existing allowance increases the credit loss.

questions.q: When a company converts a trade account receivable into a formal note receivable, the trade A/R balance:
questions.a: Decreases
questions.why: The balance moves out of trade receivables into notes receivable, reducing trade A/R.

questions.q: The cost of purchased inventory includes:
questions.a: The invoice price less purchase discounts, plus freight-in
questions.why: Costs to bring inventory to a salable location and condition (freight-in) are capitalized; selling and period costs are not.

questions.q: Under the lower-of-cost-and-net-realizable-value rule, inventory is written down when:
questions.a: Net realizable value falls below cost
wrong: Cost falls below net realizable value
questions.why: Inventory is reported at the lower of cost or NRV; a decline in NRV below cost triggers a write-down in that period.

questions.q: Inventory costing $60,000 has a net realizable value of $9,000. The write-down recognized is:
questions.why: The loss equals cost less NRV: $60,000 − $9,000 = $51,000.

questions.q: Obsolete inventory written off at year-end should be removed at the units':
questions.a: Cost
questions.why: Inventory write-offs reduce the carrying amount, which is cost — not the retail or selling price.

questions.q: A company buys 100 units at $8 and 300 units at $9. Under the weighted-average method, the cost per unit is:
questions.why: Weighted-average cost = total cost ÷ total units = $3,500 ÷ 400 = $8.75.

questions.q: Freight-in on purchased inventory that was mistakenly recorded as freight expense should be:
questions.a: Reclassified into inventory (capitalized)
wrong: Recorded as cost of goods sold
questions.why: Freight-in is a cost of acquiring inventory and is capitalized as part of inventory cost.

questions.q: Routine repainting of a building's interior should be:
questions.a: Expensed as ordinary repairs and maintenance
wrong: Added to the cost of land
questions.why: Ordinary maintenance that neither extends the asset's life nor improves it is expensed as incurred.

questions.q: Replacing an old HVAC system with a more efficient one that extends the building's life should be:
questions.a: Capitalized and depreciated, removing the old unit's cost and accumulated depreciation
wrong: Added to the cost of land
questions.why: An expenditure that extends life or improves efficiency is a capital improvement, not a repair.

questions.q: Land is acquired for $400,000; the buyer also pays $18,000 in legal and title fees and $90,000 to demolish an old building, and receives $25,000 from salvaged materials. The capitalized cost of the land is:
questions.why: Costs to ready land for use (legal fees, demolition) are capitalized; salvage proceeds reduce the cost: $400,000 + $18,000 + $90,000 − $25,000.

questions.q: Land improvements such as parking lots and fences are:
questions.a: Recorded separately from land and depreciated
wrong: Added to the cost of land
questions.why: Land itself is not depreciated, but land improvements have finite lives and are depreciated separately.

questions.q: For a self-constructed asset, the interest capitalized for the period equals:
questions.a: The lower of avoidable interest or actual interest incurred
wrong: All interest incurred on every loan
Avoidable interest even if it exceeds actual interest
questions.why: Capitalized interest is the avoidable interest, but it can never exceed the total actual interest incurred during the period.

questions.q: A company trades an old machine (book value $20,000) plus $3,000 cash for a new machine with a fair value of $25,000; the exchange lacks commercial substance. The new machine is recorded at:
questions.a: $23,000, with no gain recognized
questions.why: When an exchange lacks commercial substance and cash (boot) is paid, no gain is recognized; the new asset is recorded at the old asset's book value plus cash given ($20,000 + $3,000).

questions.q: Which depreciation method ignores salvage value when computing the periodic charge (until the salvage floor is reached)?
questions.a: Double-declining balance
questions.why: DDB applies a constant rate to declining book value without subtracting salvage; the asset is simply not depreciated below salvage.

questions.q: Equipment costs $60,000 (including installation), has a $4,000 salvage value and a 10-year life. Annual straight-line depreciation is:
questions.why: Straight-line = (cost − salvage) ÷ life = ($60,000 − $4,000) ÷ 10 = $5,600.

questions.q: An asset costs $500,000 with a 5-year life; double-declining balance is used. Year 2 depreciation is:
questions.why: The DDB rate is 2 × 20% = 40%; year 1 is $200,000, leaving $300,000, so year 2 is $120,000 (salvage is ignored in the base).

questions.q: Under sum-of-the-years'-digits for a 5-year asset, the fraction applied to the depreciable base in year two is:
questions.why: With 5 years the digits sum to 15; year two uses the next-highest digit, 4, giving 4/15.

questions.q: An asset with a net book value of $30,000 is sold for $22,000. The result is:
questions.a: An $8,000 loss
questions.why: Gain or loss on disposal equals proceeds minus net book value: $22,000 − $30,000 = an $8,000 loss.

questions.q: A long-lived asset held and used is tested for impairment. Step one (the recoverability test) compares the carrying amount to:
questions.a: The sum of the undiscounted expected future cash flows
questions.why: If undiscounted future cash flows are less than the carrying amount, the asset is not recoverable and impairment is then measured.

questions.q: Once a held-and-used asset fails the recoverability test, the impairment loss equals:
questions.a: Carrying amount minus fair value
wrong: Carrying amount minus the undiscounted cash flows
questions.why: The loss is the excess of carrying amount over fair value (often the present value of future cash flows) — not the undiscounted shortfall from step one.

questions.q: To classify a long-lived asset as held for sale, among other criteria the sale must be probable and expected to complete:
questions.a: Within one year
wrong: Only after the asset is repurposed
questions.why: Held-for-sale classification requires (among other things) that the sale is probable, expected within one year, and the asset is actively marketed.

questions.q: The cost of a patent purchased from an outside party is:
questions.a: Capitalized as an intangible asset
questions.why: Externally purchased intangibles are capitalized at cost; internally generated R&D is expensed.

questions.q: Internal research and development costs are generally:
questions.a: Expensed as incurred
questions.why: R&D is expensed as incurred, except for materials or equipment with alternative future uses, which are capitalized.

questions.q: Legal fees incurred to successfully defend a patent are:
questions.a: Capitalized, increasing the patent's carrying amount
questions.why: A successful defense preserves the asset, so the fees are capitalized; an unsuccessful defense is expensed.

questions.q: A finite-life intangible such as a patent is amortized over:
questions.a: The shorter of its legal life or useful life
questions.why: Finite-life intangibles are amortized straight-line over the shorter of legal or useful life.

questions.q: A $60,000 patent with an 8-year life is acquired on April 1. Straight-line amortization for that first calendar year is:
questions.why: Annual amortization is $7,500 ($60,000 ÷ 8), prorated for 9 months (April–December): $7,500 × 9/12 = $5,625.

doctrines.d: Stockholders' Equity or Treasury Stock questions: +55 chips.
Discontinued Operations or Comprehensive Income / OCI questions: ×2 Mult.
Answer an Income Statement question then a Comprehensive Income / OCI question in a row: ×2 Mult.
Revenue Recognition or Long-Term Contracts questions: +55 chips.
Notes & Disclosures or Subsequent Events questions: ×2 Mult.
Cash & Bank Reconciliation or Receivables questions: +55 chips.
PP&E, Depreciation & Disposal, or Impairment questions: ×2 Mult.
